retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin D Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: In hypervitaminosis D, the concentrations of vitamin D metabolites, such as 25(OH)D; 24,25(OH)2D; 25,26(OH)2D; and 25(OH)D-26,23-lactone, increase significantly. [16] Abnormally high concentrations of vitamin D metabolites exceed the VDBP-binding capacity, resulting in a release of free 1,25(OH)2D. The toxicity of vitamin D is believed to stem from elevated levels of 25(OH)D and free 1,25(OH)2D concentrations, although this hypothesis has yet to be definitively proven. [16]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Testing -- Vitamin D (Cholecalciferol)
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D status is tested by using serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D concentrations. A serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D concentration that is less than 20 ng/mL represents a vitamin D deficiency and a need for supplementation. [34]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Molecular Level -- Vitamin D (Cholecalciferol)
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D3, or cholecalciferol, had a chemical structure that contains three steroid rings and an eight-carbon side chain. The structure derives from cholesterol. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Parathyroid -- Related Testing -- Vitamin D
retrieved_docs.content: Most assays measuring vitamin D test the biologically inactive form, 25-(OH)-Vitamin D, as it is the major form of vitamin D in circulation. The active metabolite, 1,25-OH-vitamin D, has a circulating half-life of 4 to 6 hours, making it difficult to interpret accurately. Vitamin D deficiency is defined by a 25-(OH)-Vitamin D level less than 20 ng/mL and insufficiency as a level between 21 to 29 ng/mL. Normal vitamin D levels range from 30 to 100 ng/mL.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin D Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Endogenously, the skin converts 7-dehydrocholesterol (provitamin D3) to cholecalciferol (vitamin D3). This reaction is catalyzed by ultraviolet B irradiation. Vitamin D3 (from dietary sources and sunlight exposure) is carried by vitamin D-binding protein (VDBP) in the serum. Vitamin D3 is subsequently converted to 25-hydroxyvitamin D3 [25(OH)D] in the liver by the enzyme vitamin D-25-hydroxylase. After formation, 25(OH)D is transported to the kidneys via VDBP. In the proximal convoluted tubules, it is converted by 25(OH)D-1-α-hydroxylase into calcitriol, also known as 1,25-dihydroxy vitamin D3 [1,25(OH)2D]. The final product, 1,25(OH)2D, is then transported to the rest of the body via VDBP, where it serves primarily to regulate calcium metabolism. [15] Vitamin D plays an essential role in calcium resorption from bone by increasing osteoclast activity.

retrieved_docs.title: Calcitriol -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: In humans, vitamin D3 is the primary form of vitamin D, synthesized from 7-dehydrocholesterol when exposed to sunlight. Vitamin D3 can also be obtained through dietary sources or supplements. Once in the bloodstream, vitamin D3 binds to vitamin D-binding protein (DBP) and is transported for activation in the liver and kidneys. In the liver, vitamin D3 undergoes conversion by enzymes in the cytochrome P450 system to form 25-hydroxyvitamin D. This molecule is further converted in the kidneys by the enzyme 1-α hydroxylase into 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D (calcitriol), the active form of vitamin D. Calcitriol, a small lipophilic molecule with superior cellular penetration, then binds to the vitamin D receptor (VDR). [15]

retrieved_docs.title: Physiology, Integument -- Function
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D synthesis: The primary sources of vitamin D are sun exposure and oral intake. With ultraviolet sunlight exposure, 7-dehydrocholesterol converts to vitamin D3 (cholecalciferol) in the skin. Cholecalciferol is then hydroxylated in the liver, then kidney into its active metabolite form, 1,25-dihydroxy vitamin D (calcitriol). [10] This metabolite ultimately leads to increased calcium absorption in the gut and is crucial for bone health.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin D Deficiency -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Since few foods, such as fatty fish livers and fortified products, are significant sources of vitamin D, the dermal synthesis of vitamin D becomes the primary source of cholecalciferol (vitamin D3). Both cholecalciferol and ergocalciferol (vitamin D2) are converted into their respective 25-hydroxy forms—25-hydroxyvitamin D2 and 25-hydroxyvitamin D3—in the liver by hepatic 25-hydroxylase. These forms are further converted into the most active vitamin D metabolite, 1,25-dihydroxyvitamin D, by the enzyme 1α-hydroxylase in the kidneys. This active form enhances calcium absorption in the intestines, promotes bone resorption, and reduces renal excretion of calcium and phosphate. [6] Cytochrome P450 mixed-function oxidases catalyze all hydroxylation steps. [7] Vitamin D deficiency can result from a range of causes, potentially disrupting one or more stages of vitamin D activation. The following are the most important factors:

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Cellular Level -- Vitamin D (Cholecalciferol)
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D is obtained mainly through UV-B-induced production in the skin. A minor amount of vitamin D is obtained through dietary intake. In the skin, vitamin D3, or cholecalciferol, is synthesized when 7-dehydrocholesterol is exposed to UV-B light from the sun. Vitamin D3 is transferred to the liver to be further metabolized. Through dietary intake, vitamin D3 is absorbed in the small intestine via incorporation into a micelle to enter the intestinal cells and incorporate into chylomicrons to leave the intestinal cells to enter the lymph system and eventually enter circulation the thoracic duct. In the liver, vitamin D3 enters a hepatocyte and is hydroxylated into 25-OH vitamin D3, which is catalyzed by the enzyme 25-hydroxylase. Then, 25-OH vitamin D3 bind to vitamin D-binding protein, or DBP, to leave the hepatocytes and be transported to the kidney for an additional hydroxylation reaction. In the kidney, 25-OH vitamin D3 is hydroxylated to 1,25-(OH)2 vitamin D3, which is catalyzed by the enzyme 1-hydroxylase. 1,25-(OH)2 vitamin D3 is also known as calcitriol, which is considered the active form of vitamin D. 1,25-(OH)2 vitamin D3 bound to DBP is released to the bone, immune cells, and liver cells. [34] [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Biochemistry, Nutrients -- Function -- Vitamin D (Cholecalciferol)
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D, in its active form 1,25-(OH)2 vitamin D3, can function as a hormone by binding to receptors on target tissues to activate a signal transduction pathway and a regulator of gene expression by binding to a nuclear receptor to affect transcription. One of the most important functions of 1,25-(OH)2 vitamin D3 is to work with parathyroid hormone to maintain blood calcium homeostasis. 1,25-(OH)2 vitamin D3 functions to increase the absorption of calcium in the small intestine and reabsorption of calcium in the kidneys in response to low blood calcium. It also works with parathyroid hormone to stimulate the resorption of calcium from the bone to increase blood calcium levels. 1,25-(OH)2 vitamin D3 can also bind to various nuclear vitamin D receptors, or VDRs, in the bones, intestines, kidneys, and skin to stimulate the transcription of genes. This process is key to osteoclast maturation. [35]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin D -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D is fat-soluble and stored in body fat. In obese individuals,  a greater amount of vitamin D is stored in fat, and less is available for biological functions. Hence obese people require larger units of vitamin D supplementation to maintain an adequate serum level of vitamin D.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin D -- Monitoring
retrieved_docs.content: The recommendation is to check the level of the circulating form of vitamin D (25-hydroxyvitamin D) at least twice a year. Once in spring, which will reflect low levels after the winter, and once in fall, which will reflect higher levels after the summer, and the dose should be adjusted accordingly. [10]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin D Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D is a fat-soluble vitamin stored in the liver and adipose tissue. It is available from exogenous sources and synthesized endogenously by the human body. The Endocrine Society clinical practice guidelines suggest that the daily requirement of vitamin D for adults aged 19 to 50 years is 600 IU/d. For those aged 50 to 70, the daily requirement is at least 600 IU/d. For those older than 70, the daily requirement is at least 800 IU/d. The maximum suggested daily requirement is 4000 IU/d for everyone older than 8 years. [14] Vitamin D is metabolically inactive and requires a series of metabolic transformations to become its active form. This includes forms acquired from external sources or through endogenous synthesis.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin D Toxicity -- Pathophysiology
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D is primarily stored in the liver. There is a negative feedback regulation of the enzyme vitamin D-25-hydroxylase in the liver; however, it is not enough to prevent toxicity. Ingesting high doses of vitamin D leads to its storage in the liver and adipose tissue, causing saturation of the vitamin D-binding receptors. This increases the concentration of many other vitamin D metabolites, especially 25(OH)D.

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin D Deficiency -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D is available in several formulations. Notably, vitamin D3 is more efficacious than vitamin D2 when used to achieve optimal 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels, making it the preferred agent for correcting deficiencies of this nutrient. [30]

retrieved_docs.title: Vitamin D -- Mechanism of Action
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D is a hormone obtained through dietary consumption and skin production. Ultraviolet B (UVB) radiation, wavelength (290 to 315 nm), converts 7-dehydrocholesterol in the skin to previtamin D. This previtamin D undergoes heat isomerization and is converted to vitamin D. Vitamin D from the skin and diet is metabolized in the liver to 25-hydroxyvitamin D (25 OH D), and 25-hydroxyvitamin D is useful in assessing vitamin D status. In the kidneys, 25 hydroxyvitamin D converts to the biologically active form: 1,25-dihydroxy vitamin D (1,25 (OH)) by the enzyme 25-hydroxyvitamin D-1 alpha-hydroxylase (CYP27B1). Renal production of 1,25-dihydroxy vitamin is under the regulation of parathyroid, calcium, and phosphorus levels. [7]

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2030
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D and analogues is a pharmacological class that encompasses both natural vitamin D2 (ergocalciferol) and synthetic compounds such as dihydrotachysterol; dihydrotachysterol is identified as a member of the Vitamin D and analogues group, and vitamin D2 is likewise included within this broader category. This classification highlights the relationship between the natural form of vitamin D2 and its analogues, informing clinicians about the spectrum of agents that share similar biological activity and can be considered when selecting vitamin D–related therapies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2252
retrieved_docs.content: Kalcifediol and dihydrotachysterol are both classified as members of the Vitamin D and analogues group, indicating that they share the structural and functional characteristics typical of vitamin D derivatives. This classification highlights their related pharmacologic actions, therapeutic uses, and metabolic pathways within the broader context of vitamin D–related compounds.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 747
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D and analogues is a pharmacologic class that includes the specific compound dihydrotachysterol and is considered a member of the vitamin D₂ subgroup. Both Vitamin A, plain and the broader group Vitamin D and analogues are classified under the overarching category “vitamin A and D, incl. combinations of the two,” reflecting their inclusion in a combined vitamin A/ D taxonomy used for nutritional and therapeutic grouping.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 408
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin D and analogues represent a therapeutic class that includes dihydrotachysterol as one of its members. Both the Vitamin D and analogues group and the plain form of Vitamin A are classified under the broader category “vitamin A and D, incl. combinations of the two,” which groups together individual vitamins A and D as well as their combined formulations. This hierarchical organization highlights that dihydrotachysterol is a specific analogue within the Vitamin D family, while Vitamin A, plain, and the entire Vitamin D and analogues class share a common higher‑level classification that is relevant for understanding their nutritional roles, potential combined use, and therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 28 -- Level 2 -- Community 862
retrieved_docs.content: Steroid hormone is positioned within the broader category “Hormones and their metabolites and precursors,” which serves as its parent class. Within the steroid hormone lineage, kalcifediol is identified as a child compound, linking it directly to steroid metabolism. Kalcifediol, together with dihydrotachysterol, is classified as a member of the “Vitamin D and analogues” group, and this group itself is a member of the vitamin D2 subclass. Both “Vitamin D and analogues” and “Vitamin A, plain” are further defined as instances of the overarching category “vitamin a and d, incl. combinations of the two,” indicating that they belong to a combined vitamin A‑vitamin D family that includes individual vitamins as well as their combined formulations. This hierarchical structure clarifies how steroid‑derived compounds such as kalcifediol relate to vitamin D analogues, how these analogues fit within the vitamin D2 family, and how both vitamin D and vitamin A entities are grouped under a unified vitamin A/D classification, informing their metabolic relationships and potential therapeutic considerations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 2056
retrieved_docs.content: Steroid hormone encompasses kalcitriol as a subordinate entity, indicating that kalcitriol is a specific steroid hormone derivative. Both kalcitriol and dihydrotachysterol are classified as members of the group Vitamin D and analogues, reflecting their shared chemical nature as vitamin D–related compounds. The category Vitamin D and analogues, together with Vitamin A, plain, are each identified as belonging to the broader classification “vitamin A and D, incl. combinations of the two,” which groups individual vitamins and their analogues that involve either vitamin A, vitamin D, or combined formulations. This hierarchy highlights the relationship of certain steroid‑derived vitamin D analogues to the larger vitamin A/ D family, aiding clinicians in understanding how these agents are organized within pharmacologic and nutritional contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 1 -- Community 154
retrieved_docs.content: E derivatives in topical formulations. Finally, vitamin A (plain) and vitamin D (and analogues) are grouped under “vitamin A and D, including combinations of the two,” while vitamin B₁ (plain) belongs to the class “vitamin B₁, plain and in combination with vitamin B₆ and B₁₂,” reflecting the availability of both single‑nutrient and combination products. This structured representation integrates nutrient taxonomy, mineral‑vitamin interactions, deficiency states, and related pharmacologic agents, providing a comprehensive framework for patient nutrition and therapeutic management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 2031
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin A, plain is classified as a type of vitamin a and d, incl. combinations of the two, indicating that this plain form of vitamin A belongs to the broader category of formulations that contain vitamin A, vitamin D, or their combined preparations.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 2 -- Community 751
retrieved_docs.content: Dihydrotachysterol is classified as a member of the Vitamin D and analogues group, indicating that it is a vitamin D‑related compound used in clinical practice to mimic or modify the actions of natural vitamin D.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2179
retrieved_docs.content: Kalcitriol and dihydrotachysterol are both classified as members of the “Vitamin D and analogues” group, indicating that they are vitamin D–related compounds that share similar biological activity in regulating calcium and phosphate metabolism. This classification highlights their common therapeutic role as vitamin D analogues used to treat conditions such as hypocalcemia, secondary hyperparathyroidism, and certain bone disorders.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 3 -- Community 1760
retrieved_docs.content: Steroid hormones encompass the specific compounds kalcifediol and kalcitriol, indicating that these metabolites are classified as children of the broader steroid hormone family. At the same time, kalcifediol, kalcitriol, dihydrotachysterol, and vitamin D₂ are all members of the “Vitamin D and analogues” group, showing that they share common structural and functional characteristics as vitamin D–related agents. This dual classification highlights that kalcifediol and kalcitriol are both steroid‑derived hormones and vitamin D analogues, underscoring their relevance in endocrine physiology and in therapeutic contexts where vitamin D metabolism and steroid hormone activity intersect.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 1 -- Community 169
retrieved_docs.content: and vitamin D is associated with هشاشة العظام (osteoporosis), underscoring its clinical relevance to bone health. Cholekalciferol (vitamin D₃) is a steroid hormone that belongs to the broader vitamin D and analogues class and to the category “hormones, their metabolites, and precursors.” It functions as a calcium‑regulating hormone that promotes absorption of fluid and electrolytes in the small intestine, supports mineral balance and bone health, and decreases renal calcium (Ca²⁺) excretion, reflecting its role in calcium homeostasis. Cholekalciferol is linked to the disposition of the Hormone entity, may be formulated with salicylic acid (18 mg/mL) as an inactive ingredient, and can be used therapeutically in conditions such as ductal stenosis. Zinc sulfate and zinc gluconate are recognized COVID‑19 agents and are common forms of zinc‑based supplements; a multivitamin/iron capsule (FA 1 mg/IRON 106 mg) is listed as an ingredient of zinc sulfate, illustrating typical combination with

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 1088
retrieved_docs.content: Steroid hormones are included within the broader category “Hormones and their metabolites and precursors,” indicating that they are one subclass of hormonal compounds and their related metabolites. Within the steroid hormone family, the active vitamin D metabolite kalcitriol is a specific child compound, and it is classified as a member of the “Vitamin D and analogues” group. The analogue dihydrotachysterol also belongs to this same “Vitamin D and analogues” group. Both “Vitamin D and analogues” and plain vitamin A are defined as subcategories of the overarching classification “vitamin A and D, including combinations of the two,” highlighting that these nutrients and their analogues are grouped together for clinical and pharmacological considerations. This hierarchical organization clarifies how steroid‑derived vitamin D compounds relate to broader hormone categories and to the combined vitamin A/D classification used in patient care and therapeutic planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1336
retrieved_docs.content: Vitamin A, plain is a subclass that includes beta‑karoten as one of its members, while Vitamin D and analogues is a subclass that includes dihydrotachysterol as one of its members. Both Vitamin A, plain and Vitamin D and analogues are identified as types of the broader category “vitamin a and d, incl. combinations of the two.” This hierarchical organization highlights that beta‑karoten and dihydrotachysterol are specific compounds belonging to their respective vitamin groups, and that both vitamin groups are part of an overarching classification that encompasses vitamins A and D together with any combinations of the two, aiding clinicians in understanding the relationships among these vitamins and their analogues for therapeutic and nutritional planning.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 15 -- Level 3 -- Community 1334
retrieved_docs.content: Steroid hormones form a broad class of biologically active lipophilic molecules that include both calcitriol and the progestins. Calcitriol, the hormonally active form of vitamin D, is a child of the steroid hormone class, reflecting its derivation from a sterol backbone and its hormone‑like actions on calcium metabolism. Progestins, also known as progestiny, are another subclass of steroid hormones that act on progesterone receptors to regulate reproductive physiology. Megestrol acetate is a specific member of the progestin (progestiny) group, illustrating how individual synthetic agents are categorized within this steroid hormone subclass. Together, these relationships underscore the hierarchical organization of steroid hormones, linking specific agents such as calcitriol and megestrol acetate to their broader hormonal family and informing their pharmacologic and therapeutic contexts.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 31 -- Level 1 -- Community 33
retrieved_docs.content: antioxidant protection; water‑soluble B‑vitamins (niacin, calcium pantothenate) are combined with a broad spectrum of minerals and other vitamins to target cellular metabolism, energy production and vascular health. This integrated approach leverages biochemical relationships among vitamins, minerals and bioactive compounds to optimize patient health outcomes.
